title.en: The Hallucinated API: When the AI Invents Code That Doesn't Exist

description.en: LLMs don't look code up — they predict what plausible code looks like. So they confidently invent methods, parameters, and even whole libraries that never existed. Learn why it happens, the tell-tale signs, how to catch it in seconds, and the malware twist called slopsquatting.

story_variants.en:
## The Method That Was Too Convenient

You ask the AI to fetch some JSON from an API. It hands you this:

```python
import requests
data = requests.get_json("https://api.example.com/users")
```

One line. Clean. Exactly the method you *wished* existed. And it doesn't. `requests` has no `get_json()`. The real code is `requests.get(url).json()` — two calls, not one. The AI didn't read the `requests` docs and make a mistake. It never read anything. It generated the most *statistically plausible* continuation of your prompt, and "a library that fetches JSON has a `get_json` method" is an extremely plausible-sounding sentence. So it wrote one into existence.

This is **API hallucination**, and it is the signature failure of AI-assisted coding.

## Why It Happens: Prediction, Not Lookup

A language model is not a search engine with a copy of the docs. It is a **pattern-completion engine**. When it writes `df.`, it is asking "what token usually comes after `df.` in code like this?" — not "what methods does this DataFrame object actually have?" Most of the time the likely token *is* a real method, because real code dominates its training. But when your situation is unusual, or the convenient method *should* exist by analogy, the model happily fills the gap with a confident invention.

The danger is that hallucinated code is **optimized to look correct.** It follows every naming convention. `df.filter_rows(age > 30)` reads better than the real, clunkier `df[df.age > 30]`. `os.path.is_file()` looks more natural than the real `os.path.isfile()` (no underscore — Python's standard library is gloriously inconsistent, and the model smooths that inconsistency away). The invention is *more* plausible than the truth.

## The Tell-Tale Signs

You can learn to feel the hallucination before you even run the code:

| Signal | What it sounds like | Example |
|--------|--------------------|---------|
| **Too convenient** | A one-liner for something you know is fiddly | `requests.get_json(url)` |
| **Suspiciously regular name** | The API "fixed" a real inconsistency | `os.path.is_file()` vs real `isfile()` |
| **Unusual parameter** | A keyword arg that's a little too helpful | `json.dumps(data, pretty=True)` |
| **Library you've never heard of** | An import that solves your exact problem | `import easyauth` |
| **Confident but uncitable** | The AI can't point to a doc page | "It's in the standard library" (it isn't) |

None of these *prove* a hallucination. They raise your suspicion enough to check.

## How to Catch It Fast

The verification ladder from your last lesson applies directly, and the top rung is almost free:

1. **Run it.** A hallucinated method dies instantly with `AttributeError: module 'requests' has no attribute 'get_json'`. A hallucinated import dies with `ModuleNotFoundError`. The interpreter is the cheapest fact-checker you will ever own.
2. **Check the official docs.** Not a blog, not another AI — the actual project documentation. Thirty seconds confirms whether `pretty=True` is a real `json.dumps` parameter (it isn't; it's `indent=2`).
3. **Ask the AI to cite the exact doc.** "Show me where `filter_rows` appears in the pandas documentation." A real API has a real URL. A hallucination produces hand-waving, a confident-but-wrong link, or a quiet correction.

## The Special Danger: Slopsquatting

Here is where a harmless typo becomes a security incident. When an AI hallucinates a *package name* — say `import requestz` or `import pandas_helper` — and you obediently run `pip install requestz`, you're trusting that name to PyPI. Attackers have noticed. They watch which fake package names LLMs commonly invent, then **register those exact names on PyPI and fill them with malware.** This is called **slopsquatting** (slop = AI-generated filler, like typosquatting but for hallucinations).

The attack is brutal because the victim does everything "right": the AI suggested the package, the install succeeded, the import worked. Nothing errored — because the malicious package was built to import cleanly and steal your credentials in the background. The lesson: **never `pip install` a package name you first learned from an AI without confirming it on the official PyPI page and checking it's the real, maintained project.**

> **Key idea:** The AI isn't lying — it has no concept of truth. It predicts plausible text, and a non-existent API is often the *most* plausible text. Treat every unfamiliar method, parameter, and package as guilty until the interpreter or the official docs prove it innocent. The cure costs ten seconds; the disease costs a 3am incident.

nodes.label: Why APIs Get Hallucinated
children: Picks the most PLAUSIBLE next token
Invents the method that SHOULD exist
Output is optimized to look correct

animation_sequence.code:
import requests

# AI's hallucinated one-liner
data = requests.get_json("https://api.example.com")

executionSteps.description: The interpreter fact-checks instantly. get_json never existed.

description.en: The invention is MORE plausible than the truth — that's exactly why it fools you.

output: Hallucinated package -> attacker registers it on PyPI.
Confirm the package on PyPI before you ever install it.

instruction.en: An AI wrote this function to count how many names in a list start with a given letter. It used a hallucinated string method, str.starts_with(), which does NOT exist in Python (the real method is str.startswith(), no underscore). Be the verifier: fix the hallucinated method call so the code runs and returns the correct count. With the given data, 3 names start with 'A'. Expected output: 3

en: Run the starter code: it dies with AttributeError: 'str' object has no attribute 'starts_with'. That's the interpreter catching the hallucination.
Python's real method has no underscore. It is str.startswith(), not str.starts_with().
Change name.starts_with(letter) to name.startswith(letter).
When unsure a method exists, check the official docs or just run it — a hallucinated method fails instantly.

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert count_starting_with(["Ada", "Alan", "Grace", "Annie", "Linus"], "A") == 3
assert count_starting_with(["bob", "Bea"], "B") == 1
assert count_starting_with([], "A") == 0
assert count_starting_with(["xyz"], "A") == 0

q.en: Why do LLMs invent methods and libraries that don't exist?

en: They have outdated copies of the documentation
They predict plausible-looking text rather than looking the real API up
They deliberately lie to test you
They only happen when the prompt has a typo

q.en: Which of these is the FASTEST way to catch a hallucinated method like requests.get_json()?

en: Run the code and watch it raise AttributeError
Ask a different AI if it looks right
Re-read the code carefully three times
Ship it and wait for a bug report

q.en: An AI tells you to 'pip install requestz' for HTTP requests. What is the real danger?

en: It will be slower than the real requests library
Attackers may have registered that hallucinated name on PyPI with malware (slopsquatting)
pip will refuse to install it
There is no danger — pip only allows safe packages

q.en: Which is a tell-tale sign that a method might be hallucinated?

en: It uses a variable name you chose
It's a suspiciously convenient one-liner that 'fixes' a real API inconsistency
It includes a comment
It is longer than five lines
